summary: O'Neil enthusiastically announces StemForums, a new Q&A platform modeled after StackOverflow and MathOverflow but covering STEM subjects beyond coding and pure math. She explains how StackOverflow's crowdsourced, vote-ranked answers have become indispensable for programmers, and predicts similar potential for other scientific fields. The post lists the twelve subject areas StemForums will cover, from statistics to physics. O'Neil expresses mild disappointment that data science and data journalism aren't separate categories but remains optimistic about the platform's potential.
key_points: StackOverflow has become essential for coders because it provides searchable, copy-paste-ready solutions that are often more reliable than asking knowledgeable friends
MathOverflow serves a similar function for mathematicians, hosting both factual Q&A and substantive discussions
StemForums aims to extend this model to twelve STEM fields including statistics, biology, chemistry, physics, and economics
The platform is still nascent with few questions and answers, but early adopters can win Springer books as incentives
money_quotes: It is exactly what you need to get stuff working, and it's better than having a friend to ask, even a highly knowledgable friend, because your friend might be busy or might not know the answer, or even if your friend knew the answer her answer isn't cut-and-paste-able.
Just yesterday I met a cool coding chick who was learning python and pandas (of course!) with the assistance of stackoverflow.
I'm super excited for this site, it has serious potential to make peoples' lives better.
key_insight: Crowdsourced Q&A platforms like StackOverflow have revolutionized how people learn and solve problems, and extending this model to broader STEM fields could democratize scientific knowledge in the same way.
